us-politics.headline: Trump Threatens Iran With Strikes, Refuses to Rule Out Ground Troops
us-politics.body:
Trump escalated pressure on Iran over Easter Sunday, posting a profanity-laced threat to bomb power plants, bridges and infrastructure if Iran does not reopen the Strait of Hormuz. He declined to rule out deploying ground troops if no deal is reached. The threats came as US forces rescued a downed service member from behind enemy lines inside Iran.

Opinion is divided on the strategy. A former Trump adviser warned the threats make a deal less likely, not more. Top Democrats called the prospect of a ground war a costly mistake. One House Republican said the only legitimate military objective would be seizing enriched uranium. A House Democrat argued Iran's grip on the Strait poses a greater strategic threat than its nuclear program.

sources.title: Trump declines to rule out ground troops if Iran doesn’t make a deal
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post
House Republican: ‘Only purpose’ for US boots on the ground in Iran is ‘to get the enriched uranium’
Former Trump adviser on president’s latest Iran threat: ‘Not going to get a deal’
House Democrat: Iran’s control of Strait of Hormuz ‘more strategically vital’ than nuclear weapon
Inside the daring rescue of airman behind enemy lines: How CIA assisted with 'deception campaign'
Trump drops Easter Sunday f-bomb in new threat to Iran
Trump vows US will strike Iran’s power plants, bridges if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened
US rescues missing service member from downed jet in Iran: Trump

us-politics.headline: Bondi Fired, Trump Dismisses Cabinet Shake-Up Fears
us-politics.body:
Trump fired Attorney General Pam Bondi and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, but told reporters not to expect broader Cabinet upheaval. The firings drew swift Democratic criticism. Senator Tim Kaine said Bondi's dismissal should serve as a warning to whoever takes the job next, suggesting the role's independence is at risk.

NPR framed the firings alongside what it described as Trump moving to walk back certain campaign commitments. The DHS firing also triggered a funding lapse at the department. House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries called on Speaker Mike Johnson to cut the House recess short and address the gap.

sources.title: Trump says to not expect Cabinet shake-up after Bondi, Noem firings
Jeffries calls on Johnson to end House recess amid DHS funding lapse
Kaine: Bondi firing ‘should be a lesson’ for next attorney general nominee
Politics chat: Trump fires Attorney General Bondi, seeks to break campaign promises

us-politics.headline: Democrats Eye 2028, Debate How to Rebuild With Voters
us-politics.body:
Arizona Senator Ruben Gallego said he is open to a 2028 presidential run, telling reporters the party has to look at every option. Representative Ro Khanna argued Democrats who refuse to engage with unconventional media figures like streamer Hasan Piker are making a strategic error that will cost the party future elections.

A separate Hill opinion piece warned that Democrats have not yet solved their affordability problem with working-class voters, and that the issue could damage the party in the midterms if left unaddressed. The internal debate reflects a party still searching for a coherent message and messenger heading into the next cycle.

sources.title: Khanna: Those saying ‘don’t engage’ with personalities like Hasan Piker ‘will cost us future elections’
Gallego on possible 2028 presidential run: ‘We have to look at it’
‘Affordability’ could eventually bite Democrats in the bum

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Cuts NASA; NATO Tensions Deepen Over Iran War
us-politics.body:
The NASA administrator defended Trump's proposed budget cuts, arguing the agency can prioritize lunar ambitions under tighter spending. Critics pushed back, warning cuts risk ceding ground to China in the space race. A White House opinion piece separately vowed the US would not abandon its moon program.

On the alliance front, Spain's decision to ban US military aircraft from its airspace over the Iran conflict is straining NATO cohesion. Analysts described the move as a sign that universal alliance commitments are fracturing under the pressure of the ongoing war. Trump's handling of both NATO and the Iran conflict is drawing scrutiny from allies and domestic critics alike.

sources.title: NASA administrator defends Trump’s proposed budget cuts
Trump roils NATO as pressure builds over Strait of Hormuz
The White House vows America will never give up the moon again
The ‘post-global’ NATO: Spain’s airspace ban signals the death of universal alliances

au-politics.headline: Racial Discrimination Probe and Aboriginal Child Ruling Hit NSW, NT
au-politics.body:
The Australian Human Rights Commission is investigating the NSW government over alleged racial discrimination, adding pressure to state authorities already facing scrutiny over Indigenous affairs. Separately, a family court has ordered an Aboriginal child returned to their remote Northern Territory community after being moved 1,700km away without proper authority.

Together, the cases highlight ongoing tensions between government agencies and Indigenous communities over autonomy, child welfare, and systemic bias. Advocates say both decisions signal courts and oversight bodies are willing to push back hard. Critics of the rulings argue child safety and administrative complexity sometimes require difficult interventions. The competing claims remain unresolved.

sources.title: Australian Human Rights Commission investigates alleged racial discrimination by NSW government
Aboriginal child moved 1,700km from remote NT community should be returned, family court rules

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Squeezes Servo Owners, Drives Used EV Demand
au-politics.body:
Australia's fuel price crunch is hitting petrol station operators hard, with some questioning whether running a servo remains viable as margins tighten and customer frustration spills over at the bowser. The pressure is reshaping consumer behaviour faster than expected.

Used electric vehicle prices are rising as buyers look to escape ongoing fuel costs. Dealers report demand outpacing supply in the secondhand EV market, with some buyers citing the fuel crisis as the decisive factor. The shift puts fresh pressure on the government's EV transition policy - critics argue infrastructure and affordability gaps still leave many Australians without a practical alternative to petrol.

sources.title: Price crunch has some servo owners asking: 'Is it really worth doing this?'
‘Doesn’t make sense to hold onto a combustion engine’: used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits

au-politics.headline: Tasmanian Forest Logged After Rare Swift Parrot Sightings Recorded
au-politics.body:
A patch of Tasmanian forest where the critically endangered swift parrot was recorded calling dozens of times has been logged, reigniting the long-running conflict between conservation groups and the timber industry. The swift parrot is one of Australia's most at-risk bird species.

Environmentalists say the logging demonstrates that existing habitat protections are failing. Industry and government have maintained that forestry operations comply with legal requirements and that broader economic interests must be weighed. The case is likely to intensify calls for stronger pre-harvest wildlife surveys and independent oversight of logging approvals in sensitive areas.

sources.title: The swift parrot’s distinctive call was recorded dozens of times in a patch of Tasmanian forest. Then the forest was logged

au-politics.headline: Cape Grim Data and EV Shift Sharpen Australia's Climate Debate
au-politics.body:
Fifty years of atmospheric data from Cape Grim in Tasmania - home to some of the cleanest air on Earth - is providing scientists with a precise record of rising greenhouse gas concentrations. Researchers say the dataset is among the most valuable climate archives in the world and underscores the pace of change since industrialisation.

The findings land as Australia's fuel crisis accelerates consumer interest in electric vehicles and renews debate about the pace of the energy transition. Supporters of faster action point to both the Cape Grim record and rising EV demand as evidence that urgency is warranted. Those cautious about rapid transition argue cost and grid readiness remain legitimate concerns for households and businesses.

sources.title: The cleanest air on Earth holds a powerful lesson about our climate
‘Doesn’t make sense to hold onto a combustion engine’: used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits

world.headline: Israel-Iran War Escalates as Missiles Strike Both Countries
world.body:
Iran fired missiles into Israel overnight, striking a residential building in Haifa and an industrial zone in the south, while Israel continued air strikes on Iranian and Lebanese targets. At least 14 people were killed in Lebanon alone. The exchanges mark a sharp escalation in what analysts now describe as an active war with no clear off-ramp.

Trump threatened Iran with "hell" in an expletive-filled social media post, warning of consequences if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed. Europe is rattled by the shipping disruption, but Trump has offered no concrete plan. Meanwhile, a downed US F-15 pilot was rescued from inside Iran in a covert operation, adding a direct American dimension to the conflict.

Iran's internet has been shut down longer than in any previous crisis, deepening public frustration. Analysts including John Mearsheimer see no signs of a quick resolution.

sources.title: Trump threatens Iran in expletive-filled social media post
Israel continues deadly attacks on Gaza while striking Iran, Lebanon
Frustration grows as Iran’s wartime internet shutdown breaks grim record
Video captures Iranian missile striking residential building in Haifa
How downed F-15 US airman was rescued inside Iran
Seriously wounded US airman rescued from Iran, Trump says
Iranian missile strikes industrial area in southern Israel
Trump threatens ‘hell’ for Iran over Hormuz Strait as deadline approaches
Mearsheimer: No signs of quick end to US-Israel war on Iran
Why an attack on Bushehr nuclear plant would be catastrophic for the Gulf
Trump offers no concrete plan as Strait of Hormuz closure shocks Europe

world.headline: Gaza War Grinds On as Christians Mark Bleak Easter
world.body:
Israel pressed on with strikes across Gaza and Lebanon over the Easter weekend, with Palestinian Christians refusing to mark the holiday as Israeli far-right minister Itamar Ben-Gvir outlined plans for a Palestinian "death row." Displacement camps in Gaza are now battling rodent infestations alongside bombardment.

In Lebanon, Christian families celebrated Easter while displaced from their homes by Israeli shelling. The Pope used his Easter address to warn that the world is growing indifferent to violence. OPEC+ voted to raise oil output but cautioned that recovery from war-related disruptions will be slow.

sources.title: Israel continues deadly attacks on Gaza while striking Iran, Lebanon
Rodent infestations and attacks spread in Gaza tent camps
‘Refuse to celebrate’: Christians in Gaza mark sombre Easter amid genocide
Israeli air strikes, shelling across Lebanon add to rising death toll
OPEC+ agrees to hike oil output, warns of slow recovery after attacks
At least 14 people killed in Israeli strikes across Lebanon
Pope warns world is becoming ‘indifferent’ to violence
Israel’s Ben-Gvir details plans for Palestinian ‘death row’
Christian Lebanese families displaced as they celebrate Easter

world.headline: Explosives Found at Russia-Europe Gas Pipeline in Hungary
world.body:
Hungary and Serbia say explosives were discovered at a Russian natural gas pipeline ahead of Hungary's upcoming election. Budapest is alleging a deliberate plot to sabotage the infrastructure. The incident raises immediate questions about energy security in Central Europe at a moment when the broader war in the Middle East is already straining global fuel supplies.

Nepal announced two-day weekends to conserve fuel as the Iran war's impact on oil flows hits Asia. The pipeline discovery adds another pressure point to an already volatile energy landscape.

sources.title: Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election
Serbia, Hungary say explosives found at Russian gas pipeline
Nepal announces two-day weekends as fuel crisis caused by Iran war deepens

world.headline: Migrant Boat Capsizes Near Libya; Bangladesh Measles Toll Rises
world.body:
Dozens of migrants are missing after a boat capsized in the Mediterranean near Libya, the latest in a long series of deadly crossings as conflict and instability push people toward the sea route into Europe. Search and rescue operations are under way.

Separately, a suspected measles outbreak in Bangladesh has killed nearly 100 children, raising alarm about vaccine coverage gaps. The two crises - one unfolding at sea, one in a hospital system - reflect the compounding humanitarian pressures building outside the main war zones.

sources.title: Dozens missing after boat carrying migrants capsizes near Libya
Dozens missing after migrant boat capsizes in Mediterranean
Suspected measles outbreak kills nearly 100 children in Bangladesh

ai.headline: Google's Gemma 4 Lands on iPhones and Local Machines
ai.body:
Google's Gemma 4 model is now running on consumer hardware, with an official iOS app appearing on the App Store and developers already documenting how to run it locally using LM Studio's headless CLI alongside Claude Code. The speed of adoption signals growing appetite for on-device inference that bypasses cloud costs entirely.

Meanwhile, a developer published Nanocode, a pure JAX implementation targeting TPUs, framing it as the best Claude Code experience $200 can buy. Gemini is also showing up in more consumer products - a hands-on with Gemini inside Google Maps found the day-planning feature genuinely useful, a rare positive verdict in a space full of overpromised AI integrations.

sources.title: Gemma 4 on iPhone
Running Google Gemma 4 Locally with LM Studio's New Headless CLI and Claude Code
Nanocode: The best Claude Code that $200 can buy in pure JAX on TPUs
I let Gemini in Google Maps plan my day and it went surprisingly well

ai.headline: Microsoft Calls Copilot 'Entertainment Only' in Fine Print
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of use now classify Copilot as being for entertainment purposes only, a legal hedge that directly contradicts how the product is marketed to businesses and consumers. The framing limits Microsoft's liability if the AI produces wrong or harmful outputs, but it also quietly undermines the case for using it in any serious context.

The disclosure lands as AI legal exposure grows across the industry. Suno faces mounting scrutiny over music copyright, with critics arguing its AI-generated covers create a nightmare for rights holders. Together, both stories illustrate how companies are racing to deploy AI products while quietly distancing themselves from accountability for what those products actually do.

sources.title: Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use
Suno is a music copyright nightmare

ai.headline: Japan's Robot Workforce Fills Jobs, Not Headlines
ai.body:
Japan is deploying physical AI robots not to replace workers but to fill roles that go unfilled - a distinction that matters in a country with acute labor shortages and an aging population. TechCrunch reports that experimental physical AI is proving ready for real-world conditions, with robots handling tasks in logistics, hospitality, and care that human workers simply aren't available to do.

The story cuts against the dominant Western narrative of automation as displacement. In Japan, the pressure runs the other way. Robots are a stopgap, not a threat, and the country is becoming a live test bed for whether physical AI can scale outside controlled environments.

sources.title: In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants

ai.headline: SpaceX Eyes Orbital Data Centers to Justify Its Valuation
ai.body:
SpaceX is exploring orbital data centers as a potential revenue stream, a concept that would place computing infrastructure in low Earth orbit to serve latency-sensitive or energy-constrained use cases. TechCrunch examines whether the idea holds enough commercial weight to support the company's enormous private valuation.

The pitch is speculative but not absurd. Starlink already gives SpaceX a functioning orbital network, and the AI infrastructure boom has made data center capacity a genuine constraint on Earth. Whether orbital compute ever pencils out economically is unproven. But the conversation itself reflects how far the industry is willing to reach for the next edge in AI infrastructure.

sources.title: Can orbital data centers help justify a massive valuation for SpaceX?

btc.headline: Bitcoin Holds at $67K as Sentiment Craters
btc.body:
Bitcoin is stuck near $67,000 with market sentiment at its worst levels since the Iran war began, yet the price refuses to break down. Traders are calling new lows a matter of time, but the resilience is notable given the backdrop.

One structural shift may explain the stubbornness. ETF inflows have rewired how Bitcoin responds to macro signals - it is now front-running Federal Reserve moves rather than reacting to them, as institutional money positions ahead of policy pivots. A Bitcoin Policy Institute executive added another layer, arguing Bitcoin and the US dollar share a symbiotic relationship rather than a zero-sum one. Robert Kiyosaki, meanwhile, is beating his familiar drum, pointing to a 1974-era monetary shift as reason to hold Bitcoin and gold.

sources.title: Bitcoin holds steady as sentiment hits worst levels since Iran war began
New Bitcoin price lows 'matter of time' says trader with BTC stuck at $67K
Bitcoin is now front-running the Fed rather than reacting to it. ETFs are the cause
Bitcoin and the US dollar have a 'symbiotic' relationship: BPI exec
Robert Kiyosaki recommends Bitcoin, gold as 1974 shift comes full circle

btc.headline: North Korea Ran Six-Month Operation Behind $270M Drift Hack
btc.body:
The $270 million exploit of Solana-based trading platform Drift was not a smash-and-grab. The platform says North Korean intelligence agents spent six months embedded in the operation before executing the theft, marking one of the most sophisticated state-sponsored crypto heists on record.

The attack lands as Ledger's CTO warns that AI is actively making crypto security worse, lowering the cost and skill threshold for attackers to craft phishing campaigns and exploit vulnerabilities. Together, the two stories paint a grim picture: threats are growing more patient and more automated at the same time.

sources.title: Drift says $270 million exploit was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation
AI is making crypto's security problem even worse, Ledger CTO warns

btc.headline: Quantum Computing Threat to Bitcoin Is Real and Growing
btc.body:
A new explainer from CoinDesk cuts through the noise on quantum computing and lands on an uncomfortable conclusion - the threat to Bitcoin's cryptographic foundations is genuine, not theoretical. Quantum machines capable of breaking elliptic curve encryption could expose private keys, putting older or reused Bitcoin addresses at particular risk.

The timeline remains uncertain, but the direction is not. The Bitcoin development community has been slow to coalesce around post-quantum cryptography standards, and the window to act before capable machines arrive is narrowing. It is a slow-moving crisis that the market is largely ignoring.

sources.title: A simple explainer on what quantum computing actually is, and why it is terrifying for bitcoin

btc.headline: Token Oversupply, Polymarket Backlash, and AI on Crypto Rails
btc.body:
A Blockworks executive called token oversupply an existential problem for crypto, arguing that new issuance is outpacing any real value creation and slowly poisoning the broader market. The diagnosis is blunt: too many tokens chasing too little utility.

Polymarket pulled a set of prediction markets tied to Iran rescue scenarios after a sharp public backlash, highlighting the persistent tension between permissionless markets and ethical limits. On a more forward-looking note, Ant Group's blockchain arm unveiled a platform letting AI agents transact autonomously on crypto rails - a sign that the AI-crypto convergence is moving from concept to infrastructure.

sources.title: ‘Existential’ token problem seen as supply outpaces value creation: Blockworks exec
Polymarket pulls controversial Iran rescue markets after intense backlash
Ant Group’s blockchain arm unveils platform for AI agents to transact on crypto rails

food.name: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
food.description: Halved aubergines broiled until silky and caramelised under a sweet-savoury miso and mirin glaze
food.tip: Score the flesh in a crosshatch before glazing — it lets the miso penetrate deeper and speeds up cooking by about a third

exercise.name: The Sunday Staircase Pyramid
exercise.equipment: A staircase (indoor or outdoor) and a timer
exercise.description: Climb and descend stairs in timed intervals that build from 30 seconds to 3 minutes and back down, turning a mundane structure into a genuine cardio and leg-strength session

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to human cells, memories, and beliefs, it becomes a surprisingly sharp question about whether you are the same person you were ten years ago.
learn.why: On a quiet Sunday it's a rare chance to sit with a problem that has no clean answer, and philosophers from Heraclitus to Derek Parfit have left genuinely unsettling breadcrumbs worth following
